clean_story:
Raw strength. Hidden leverage.

The word might comes from Old English miht, meaning power—not just muscle, but the force behind a river carving stone or a single idea toppling empires.

Might isn’t brute size. A water strider’s legs distribute its weight across surface tension, letting it walk on liquid like a invisible bridge. A honeybee’s wings beat 200 times per second, generating lift without bones—just chitin and precision. Humans wield might differently: a lever turns 10 pounds of effort into 100 pounds of lift; a well-placed wedge splits boulders with a tap. Tools multiply force, but strategy decides where to apply it.

The strongest systems often hide their effort. Ants carry 50x their weight by sharing loads in chains. Wolves hunt in relays, trading exhaustion for persistence. Even language has might—a single rumor can uproot markets; a law rewrites what millions can do.

Three related subjects are force multipliers, collective power dynamics, and how limits shape strength.
